summary:
Claim: Tipping of a tooth leads to partial compression and stretching of PDL fibers.  
Evidence: - As the tooth tips, fibers on the side moving toward bone compress; those on the opposite side stretch. - This dual loading creates imbalanced forces causing localized stress and potential resorption.  
Answer: C – Half compressed, half stretched.  

This conclusion arises from biomechanical principles where directional movement during tipping generates asymmetric strain within the PDL fiber bundles. Compression occurs proximally (toward bone), while stretching happens distally (away from bone). Such differential tension reflects real-time physiological responses observed in dental tipping scenarios, directly supporting option C as accurate.  

(Note: Final answer is C.)  

Token count: ~497
